summary: Cantrill reflects on a small victory when a tech journalist corrected factual errors in a Solaris 10 story after Cantrill complained on his blog. He uses the experience to consider the difficult position of technical reporters who must translate complex information from fast-talking engineers while readers expect perfect accuracy. The post acknowledges both sides of the accuracy problem: engineers who dump technical detail and expect perfection, and readers who blame technology rather than journalism when details are wrong.
key_points: Complaining publicly on a blog led to a journalist correcting technical errors
Technical reporters face the challenge of dealing with fast-talking engineers who expect absolute accuracy
Readers assume technical articles are accurate and blame the technology or technologist when details are wrong
There's a tension between the difficulty of technical reporting and the consequences of inaccuracy
money_quotes: It must take a thick skin to be dealing with people like me all of the time: fast talkers who bombard with technical detail, and then expect absolute accuracy in whatever stories emerge.

The problem is that the readership assumes absolute accuracy – and if the technical details are incorrect, they will naturally blame the technology (or worse, the technologist) instead of questioning the accuracy of the reportage.
key_insight: Technical inaccuracy in journalism creates an unfair attribution problem where the technologist, not the reporter, bears the reputational cost of errors.
